course.title: Intro to React
course.description: Master the fundamentals of React — components, state, props, and hooks — through bite-sized scrollable lessons.

content.beginner: React is a JavaScript library created by Facebook for building user interfaces. Think of it like LEGO — you build small, reusable pieces called **components**, and snap them together to build a full UI.
content.intermediate: React is a declarative, component-based library for building UIs. It uses a virtual DOM to efficiently compute minimal updates to the real DOM, making re-renders fast.
content.advanced: React implements a unidirectional data flow with a reconciliation algorithm (Fiber) that can pause, abort, and restart rendering work. It separates the rendering phase (pure, side-effect free) from the commit phase (DOM mutations).

challenge.statement: React is a JavaScript framework created by Google.
challenge.explanation: React is a library (not a framework) and was created by Facebook (Meta). The distinction matters — a library you call, a framework calls you. React handles UI; you choose the rest of the stack.

content.advanced: React components must be pure with respect to their props — given the same props, they must always return the same JSX. Side effects belong in `useEffect`. Each component instance maintains its own fiber node in the work-in-progress tree.

challenge.statement: React component function names must start with a capital letter.
challenge.explanation: React uses the capital letter to tell the difference between a component and an HTML tag. Writing <button> renders an HTML button; writing <Button> renders your component. This is enforced by the JSX transform.

content.intermediate: JSX is syntactic sugar over `React.createElement()`. You can embed any JavaScript expression inside `{}`. JSX attributes use camelCase (`className` not `class`, `onClick` not `onclick`).
content.advanced: JSX compiles to `React.createElement(type, props, ...children)` calls. With the new JSX transform (React 17+), you no longer need `import React` in scope — the transform auto-imports from `react/jsx-runtime`.

challenge.explanation: JSX is syntactic sugar for React.createElement(). Babel converts every JSX tag into this function call at build time — JSX itself never reaches the browser.

content.intermediate: Props flow one way: parent → child (unidirectional data flow). Props are read-only — a component must never modify its own props. For communication upward, pass callback functions as props.
content.advanced: Props can be any JavaScript value — primitives, objects, arrays, functions, even other React elements (`children`). The `children` prop is implicit for content between component tags. Use `React.FC<Props>` for TypeScript typing.

challenge.statement: A React component is allowed to modify its own props.
challenge.explanation: Props are read-only — always. They flow one way: parent to child. If a component needs to communicate back upward, the parent passes a callback function down as a prop instead.

content.intermediate: `useState` returns a tuple: `[currentValue, setter]`. Calling the setter triggers a re-render. State updates are **asynchronous and batched** in React 18+. Use the functional updater form `setCount(c => c + 1)` when the new state depends on old state.
content.advanced: `useState` is built on `useReducer` internally. State is stored in the fiber node, not the component function's closure. React uses the **hook call order** (not names) to associate state with the correct hook call — this is why hooks can't be called conditionally.

challenge.explanation: useState is the hook that adds local state to a function component. It takes the initial value and returns a tuple — the current state and a setter function that triggers a re-render.

content.intermediate: React uses synthetic events — a cross-browser wrapper around native events. Event handlers receive a `SyntheticEvent` object. React 17+ changed event delegation from `document` to the React root container.
content.advanced: Synthetic events are pooled and reused in React 16 (not in React 17+). Event handler closures capture state values from the render they were created in — this is the 'stale closure' problem. Use `useRef` or functional state updates to avoid it.

challenge.explanation: React uses camelCase event names — onClick not onclick. You pass the function reference directly. Writing onClick={handleClick()} would call it immediately on every render instead of waiting for a click.

content.intermediate: Three patterns: `if/else`, ternary `condition ? <A/> : <B/>`, and short-circuit `condition && <A/>`. The short-circuit pattern can render `0` to the screen if the condition is a falsy number — use `!!count &&` or `count > 0 &&` to avoid this.
content.advanced: React can render `null`, `undefined`, `false`, and empty arrays — none of these produce DOM nodes. Arrays of elements are valid JSX and require unique `key` props. Avoid using indexes as keys when list items can reorder.

challenge.statement: The expression {0 && <Component />} renders nothing to the screen.
challenge.explanation: The number 0 is falsy, but React renders it as the literal character '0'. Only false, null, undefined, and empty strings render nothing. Use {count > 0 && <Component />} to safely short-circuit.

content.intermediate: Keys must be stable, unique among siblings. Using array index as key causes bugs when items reorder or are inserted/deleted — React will incorrectly reuse DOM nodes. Prefer database IDs or stable unique strings.
content.advanced: Keys are React's reconciliation hint. During diffing, if a key doesn't match, React destroys the old component and mounts a new one (resetting state). Keys work per-list — the same key can appear in different lists. Keys are not passed as props — use a separate `id` prop if needed.

challenge.explanation: Every element in a .map() list needs a unique key prop so React can track which items changed, were added, or removed. Without it you'll get a console warning — and subtle rendering bugs.

content.intermediate: The `useEffect` dependency array controls when the effect runs: `[]` = once on mount, `[val]` = when `val` changes, no array = every render. Always include all values from the component scope that the effect uses — the `exhaustive-deps` ESLint rule enforces this.
content.advanced: Effects run after paint (asynchronous). Return a cleanup function to cancel subscriptions, abort fetches, or clear timers — it runs before the next effect and on unmount. In React 18 Strict Mode, effects run twice in development to help detect missing cleanups.

challenge.explanation: The dependency array tells React when to re-run the effect. Listing id here means 're-fetch whenever the id prop changes'. An empty array [] means run once on mount only.

content.beginner:
Think of a React component like a plant:
- **Mount** = seed sprouting (component appears on screen)
- **Update** = plant growing (state/props change, re-render)
- **Unmount** = plant removed (component leaves the screen)

`useEffect` lets you hook into each stage.
content.intermediate: Functional components with hooks map to lifecycle: mount → `useEffect(fn, [])`, update → `useEffect(fn, [dep])`, unmount → cleanup function returned from `useEffect`. Class component methods (`componentDidMount`, `componentDidUpdate`, `componentWillUnmount`) are now discouraged.
content.advanced: React 18's concurrent features mean components can mount, unmount, and remount (e.g. Offscreen/Activity API). This breaks code that assumes mount = once. Strict Mode's double-mount in development simulates this. Effects must be idempotent — running them twice should produce the same result.

challenge.statement: useEffect with an empty dependency array [] runs after every re-render.
challenge.explanation: useEffect with [] runs exactly once — after the component first mounts. It's the functional equivalent of componentDidMount. No dependency array at all (omitting it) is what runs after every render.

content.intermediate: Custom hooks let you share stateful logic between components without changing the component tree (no render props or HOCs needed). Each component that calls a custom hook gets its own isolated state — hooks don't share state, just logic.
content.advanced: Custom hooks are the primary abstraction unit in modern React. They compose: hooks can call other hooks. The `use` prefix is a lint-enforced convention that enables ESLint rules (like `exhaustive-deps`) to apply to your custom hook. Hooks can even wrap async functions using the new `use()` hook for Suspense integration.

challenge.explanation: The 'use' prefix is both a naming convention and a requirement. React's ESLint plugin uses it to enforce rules-of-hooks on your custom hooks. useWindowWidth, useAuth, useLocalStorage — always start with 'use'.

content.intermediate: Context is not a state management solution — it's a dependency injection mechanism. Every component that consumes a Context will re-render when the context value changes. For large apps, split contexts by update frequency to minimize unnecessary re-renders.
content.advanced: Context uses object identity for change detection — passing `value={{ user, setUser }}` creates a new object every render, causing all consumers to re-render. Memoize the value with `useMemo`. For complex state, pair Context with `useReducer` or use Zustand/Jotai for fine-grained subscriptions.

challenge.explanation: createContext() creates a Context object with a default value. You then wrap part of your tree in ThemeContext.Provider to supply a value, and any descendant can read it with useContext — no prop drilling needed.

content.intermediate: `useMemo` and `useCallback` are optimizations — only use them when you have a measured performance problem. Overusing them adds overhead (memoization has a cost) and makes code harder to read. Profile first with React DevTools Profiler.
content.advanced: In React Compiler (React 19+), the compiler automatically memoizes components and values — `useMemo`/`useCallback` become largely unnecessary. Until then, they're critical for: (1) referential stability for context values, (2) preventing child re-renders wrapped with `React.memo`, (3) stable deps arrays for `useEffect`.

challenge.statement: You should wrap every computed value in useMemo to maximize performance.
challenge.explanation: useMemo has overhead — it allocates memory to store the cached result and runs a comparison on every render. Only use it when you've profiled and confirmed a genuine performance problem. Premature optimization makes code harder to read for no gain.

content.intermediate: Controlled vs uncontrolled inputs: controlled = React owns the value (via state + onChange), uncontrolled = DOM owns the value (access via `useRef`). Controlled is preferred for validation and computed values. `e.preventDefault()` stops the native form submission.
content.advanced: For complex forms, use React Hook Form — it uses uncontrolled inputs under the hood (via `register` and refs) to avoid re-renders on every keystroke. It integrates with Zod for schema validation. React 19's `useActionState` and form Actions provide native async form handling with Suspense.

challenge.explanation: A controlled input pairs value (what React displays) with onChange (how React updates when the user types). Without onChange, the input becomes read-only — the value is stuck at whatever state holds.

content.beginner:
You've learned the core of React:
✅ Components & JSX
✅ Props & State
✅ Events & Conditional Rendering
✅ Lists & Keys
✅ useEffect
✅ Custom Hooks
✅ Context API

Next steps: Try building a todo app, then explore **Next.js** for full-stack React!
content.intermediate:
You've covered the fundamentals. Next, explore:
- **React Router** for SPAs
- **TanStack Query** for server state
- **Zustand** for client state
- **Next.js** for SSR/SSG
- **Vitest + Testing Library** for testing
content.advanced:
From here, dive into:
- **React Compiler** (React 19)
- **Server Components** and the `use` hook
- **Concurrent features**: `useDeferredValue`, `useTransition`
- **Suspense** boundaries and streaming SSR
- **React Native** for mobile

challenge.statement: Custom React hooks can call other hooks inside them.
challenge.explanation: Hooks compose — that's what makes them powerful. A custom hook is just a function that follows the rules of hooks, including being able to call useState, useEffect, and other custom hooks. You can build arbitrarily deep hook trees.

options: A reusable function that returns JSX
questions.explanation: React components are functions (or classes) that return JSX, describing what should appear on screen.

options: An array with the value and a setter function
questions.explanation: useState returns a tuple: [currentValue, setterFunction]. You destructure it to get both.

questions.questionText: Which is the correct way to pass a prop?
questions.explanation: Pass the function reference (not a call) using camelCase: onClick={handleClick}. Adding () would call it immediately on render.

questions.questionText: When does a useEffect with [id] in the dependency array run?
options: Only once when the component mounts
questions.explanation: The dependency array tells React when to re-run the effect. [id] means 'run this when id changes'.

questions.questionText: What is the problem with using array indexes as list keys?
options: Keys must be numbers, not strings
It causes bugs when items reorder or are inserted/deleted
questions.explanation: Index-based keys confuse React's reconciler when the list changes order — it incorrectly reuses DOM nodes and can reset component state.

options: Passing props through many component levels just to reach a deep child
A way to drill into DOM nodes
questions.explanation: Prop drilling is when you pass props through intermediate components that don't need them, just to get data to a deeply nested child. Context solves this.

questions.questionText: What is a controlled input in React?
options: An input whose value is controlled by the DOM
An input whose value is controlled by React state
An input with a required attribute
questions.explanation: A controlled input has its value driven by React state, and uses an onChange handler to update that state.

options: When you have a measured performance problem with an expensive computation
questions.explanation: useMemo is an optimization tool. Use it only when you've profiled and identified a real performance issue — premature optimization adds overhead.

questions.questionText: What does a custom hook name must start with?
questions.explanation: Custom hooks must start with 'use' (e.g. useWindowWidth). This is both a convention and required for ESLint's rules-of-hooks to apply.
